meta.title: 5.1 TypeScript Is Just Labels
meta.description: TypeScript adds labels to data -- what type each variable is. It looks scary but it is just documentation.

blocks.prompt: You have seen JavaScript variables like `const name = "Ben"`. In TypeScript, you might see `const name: string = "Ben"`. What do you think the `: string` part does? Does it change the behavior, or is it something else?

blocks.question: Given `interface Product { name: string; price: number; inStock?: boolean; }`, is a Product object required to have an `inStock` property?
blocks.answer: No. The `?` after `inStock` means it is optional. A valid Product object needs `name` and `price`, but `inStock` can be left out.

options: A) `users` is an array of User objects, each with an id (number), name (text), and role (one of three specific strings)
B) `users` is a single User object with id, name, and role properties
C) `users` is a function that returns User objects
problems.explanation: `User[]` means an array of User objects. The interface defines the shape: `id` is a number, `name` is a string, and `role` must be exactly one of "admin", "editor", or "viewer". The `|` creates a union type -- it can only be one of those values.

options: A) It requires a number and a string, and returns a number
B) It requires a number, optionally accepts a string, and returns a string
C) It requires two strings and returns a string
problems.explanation: `amount: number` is required. `currency?: string` has the `?`, so it is optional. The return type after the closing parenthesis is `: string`. You can call this as `formatPrice(9.99)` or `formatPrice(9.99, "EUR")`.

options: A) The dashboard needs a title and metrics. Alerts and comparison period are required too.
B) The dashboard needs a title, a metrics object with three numbers, and a lastUpdated string. Alerts (an array of strings) and comparisonPeriod (one of three values) are optional.
C) The dashboard only needs a title. Everything else is optional.
problems.explanation: Properties without `?` are required: `title`, `metrics` (which itself has three required number properties), and `lastUpdated`. Properties with `?` are optional: `alerts` (an array of strings) and `comparisonPeriod` (which can be "week", "month", or "quarter"). This interface IS the documentation for what this dashboard component needs.

blocks.prompt: In your own words, explain what TypeScript adds to JavaScript. If you were telling a colleague who had never seen TypeScript, how would you describe what the colons and interface blocks do?

blocks.content: TypeScript is JavaScript with labels. The colon introduces a type annotation -- it documents what kind of data a variable holds, a function expects, or a function returns. Interfaces define the shape of objects. The `?` marks optional properties. None of this changes behavior -- it is all documentation that helps you (and the editor) understand the code.

blocks.prompt: Think about a spreadsheet with column headers like "Name (text)", "Price (number)", "In Stock (yes/no)". TypeScript interfaces work the same way -- they are column headers for your data. Next time you see an interface, try reading it like a table schema.

reviewQuestions.question: What does the '?' mean after a property name in a TypeScript interface?
options: The property must be a boolean
The property is optional -- it may or may not exist
The property has a default value
reviewQuestions.explanation: A question mark after a property name (like phone?: string) means the property is optional. A valid object may include it or leave it out.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: That's right! The ? is TypeScript's way of saying 'this might not be there.'
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The ? marks a property as optional -- it might be present or might be undefined. Required properties have no ?.

reviewQuestions.question: What does TypeScript add to JavaScript?
options: Type annotations that document what kind of data variables hold
reviewQuestions.explanation: TypeScript adds type annotations -- labels that document data types. It does not change what the code does at runtime. The types are like labels on boxes that tell you what is inside.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Spot on! Types are documentation, not logic. They help you understand the code without changing its behavior.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: TypeScript adds type annotations -- labels that document data. It does not change runtime behavior. Think of it as adding labels to your variables.
